content:
# Mark Chapter 12 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Mark Chapter 12 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 12:1 — "leased it to tenants" vs. "leased it to-tenant-farmers" vs. "leased the vineyard to some farmers"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "leased it to-tenant-farmers" | Literal; γεωργοῖς = farmers/tenants. |
| LOGOS | "leased it to tenants" | Formal; concise. |
| PNEUMA | "leased the vineyard to some farmers" | Dynamic; adds "some." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "leased it to tenants" — concise and accurate. "Tenant-farmers" (EMET) is unnecessarily compound; "some farmers" (PNEUMA) adds an indefinite article.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:10 — "head of the corner" vs. "cornerstone" vs. "most important stone of all"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[the] head of-[the] corner" | Literal; κεφαλὴν γωνίας. |
| LOGOS | "the cornerstone" | Traditional English rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "the most important stone of all" | Dynamic; explains the concept. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the cornerstone" — this is the established rendering of κεφαλὴν γωνίας and is widely recognized from Psalm 118:22. PNEUMA's expansion loses the technical architectural term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:14 — "taxes" vs. "tribute" vs. "taxes" for κῆνσον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Is-it-permitted to-give tribute to Caesar" | "Tribute" for κῆνσος (Latinism: census tax). |
| LOGOS | "Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar" | "Taxes" for modern clarity. |
| PNEUMA | "is it right to pay taxes to Caesar" | Same; "right" for ἔξεστιν. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "to pay taxes to Caesar" — while κῆνσος technically refers to the specific Roman poll tax, "taxes" communicates more immediately. The context makes the political nature clear.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 12:16 — "likeness" vs. "image" vs. "portrait" for εἰκών

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Whose [is] this image and the inscription?" | εἰκών = image. |
| LOGOS | "Whose likeness and inscription is this?" | "Likeness" for εἰκών. |
| PNEUMA | "Whose portrait is this?...And whose name?" | Dynamic; separates into two questions. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Whose likeness and inscription is this?" — "likeness" (LOGOS) is a deliberate choice that connects to the Genesis 1:26-27 theme of humanity made in God's "likeness" (εἰκών). This intertextual resonance is theologically important.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The εἰκών/likeness connection to Genesis is key.

---

## 12:17 — "Give" vs. "Render" vs. "Give back" for ἀπόδοτε

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "give-back to-Caesar" | Preserves ἀποδίδωμι = give back/return. |
| LOGOS | "Render to Caesar" | Traditional; archaic. |
| PNEUMA | "Give to Caesar what belongs to Caesar" | Natural; drops "back" and "render." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Give to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's." While ἀποδίδωμι means "give back," and EMET is technically most precise, "Render" is archaic and "give back" disrupts the parallelism. "Give" serves both clauses equally and the "back" is noted in the commentary.

**Status:** Resolved. A pragmatic choice favoring PNEUMA's verb with LOGOS's structure.

---

## 12:28 — "first of all" vs. "most important of all" for πρώτη πάντων

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Which commandment is first of-all?" | Literal; πρώτη = first. |
| LOGOS | "Which commandment is the most important of all?" | Interprets "first" as "foremost." |
| PNEUMA | "Of all the commandments, which is the most important?" | Same interpretation; restructured. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the most important of all" — πρώτη here functions as "chief/foremost" rather than sequential "first."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 12:30 — "with all your heart" vs. "from your whole heart" vs. "with every ounce of your heart"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from your whole heart and from your whole soul and from your whole mind and from your whole strength" | Literal; ἐξ ὅλης = "from/out of your whole." |
| LOGOS | "with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind and with all your strength" | Formal; "with all" is standard. |
| PNEUMA | "with every ounce of your heart, soul, mind, and strength" | Dynamic; condenses; "every ounce" for emphasis. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind and with all your strength" — the fourfold repetition of "with all your" is rhetorically powerful and should be preserved in full, not condensed.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:36 — "The Lord said to my Lord" vs. "The Lord said to my lord"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The Lord said to my lord" | Lowercase for second "lord" (human addressee in Psalm 110). |
| LOGOS | "The Lord said to my Lord" | Both capitalized; christological reading. |
| PNEUMA | "The Lord said to my Lord" | Same as LOGOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "The Lord said to my Lord" — while the psalm's original context may not have intended divine reference for the second "lord," Mark's christological use (Jesus arguing the Messiah is greater than David's descendant) warrants the capitalization.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 12:38 — "long robes" vs. "flowing robes" for στολαῖς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in robes" | Simple; στολή = robe. |
| LOGOS | "in long robes" | Adds "long" for visual specificity. |
| PNEUMA | "in flowing robes" | "Flowing" for visual effect. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "in long robes" — the στολή was a distinctive formal garment, and "long" accurately conveys the visual impression without PNEUMA's more literary "flowing."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:40 — "devour widows' houses" vs. "exploit widows and rob them of their homes"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "those devouring the houses of-widows" | Literal; κατεσθίω = devour/consume. |
| LOGOS | "who devour widows' houses" | Formal; same metaphor. |
| PNEUMA | "They exploit widows and rob them of their homes" | Dynamic; replaces metaphor with plain speech. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "who devour widows' houses" — the metaphor of "devouring" is powerful and should be preserved. PNEUMA's "exploit and rob" explains but loses the vivid image.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 12:42 — Coin terminology: "two small coins, which is a quadrans" vs. "two small copper coins, worth very little"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "two small-coins, which is a-quadrans" | Preserves the Roman denomination. |
| LOGOS | "two small copper coins, which make a penny" | Converts to accessible denomination. |
| PNEUMA | "two tiny coins — practically nothing" | Dynamic; emphasizes insignificance. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "two small copper coins, worth very little" — identifies the material (copper, from λεπτά) and conveys the value without using a modern denomination ("penny") that creates false precision.

**Status:** Resolved. SOPHIA's own formulation.

---

## 12:44 — "her whole livelihood" vs. "everything she had to live on" for ὅλον τὸν βίον αὐτῆς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "her whole livelihood" | βίος = livelihood/means of living. |
| LOGOS | "her whole livelihood" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "everything she had to live on" | Dynamic; explanatory. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three convey the same meaning. Adopted "her whole livelihood" — concise and faithful.

**Status:** No significant disagreement.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **OT quotation vocabulary** (cornerstone, devour, Lord/lord) | vv. 10, 36, 40 | Traditional/recognizable terms retained |
| **Intertextual connections** (εἰκών/likeness to Genesis) | v. 16 | Word choices that preserve allusions |
| **Rhetorical structure** (fourfold "with all your") | v. 30 | Repetition preserved; condensation rejected |
| **Currency/measurement** | v. 42 | Accessible rendering without false modernization |
| **Key verbs** (ἀποδίδωμι, κατεσθίω) | vv. 17, 40 | Pragmatic choices balancing precision and readability |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Mark 12 is a chapter of verbal contests (tribute question, resurrection debate, greatest commandment, David's Lord question) followed by the quiet drama of the widow's offering. The translators show remarkably strong agreement on the contest dialogues, diverging mainly on stylistic register. The most theologically loaded decision is the rendering of εἰκών as "likeness" (v. 16), which LOGOS correctly identifies as connecting to the creation narrative. The widow's offering (vv. 41-44) shows the least divergence, with all three translators capturing the contrast between abundance and poverty effectively.
